From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Geographical database

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/USGS_logo_green.svg/250px-USGS_logo_green.svg.png)](/wiki/File:USGS_logo_green.svg)

The logo of the United States Geological Survey (USGS)

The **Geographic Names Information System** (**GNIS**) is a database of name and location information about more than two million physical and cultural features, encompassing the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"); the [associated states](/wiki/Compact_of_Free_Association "Compact of Free Association") of the [Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands"), [Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia"), and [Palau](/wiki/Palau "Palau"); and [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica"). It is a type of [gazetteer](/wiki/Gazetteer "Gazetteer"). It was developed by the [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") (USGS) in cooperation with the [United States Board on Geographic Names](/wiki/United_States_Board_on_Geographic_Names "United States Board on Geographic Names") (BGN) to promote the standardization of feature names.

Data were collected in two phases.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200830-1) A third phase was considered, which would have handled name changes where local usages differed from maps, it was never begun.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200831-2)

The database is part of a system that includes topographic map names and bibliographic references. The names of books and historic maps that confirm the feature or place name are cited. Variant names, alternatives to official federal names for a feature, are also recorded. Each feature receives a permanent, unique feature record identifier, sometimes called the GNIS identifier.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The database never removes an entry, "except in cases of obvious duplication."[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Original purposes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Original purposes")]

The GNIS was originally designed for four major purposes: to eliminate duplication of effort at various other levels of government that were already compiling geographic data, to provide standardized datasets of geographic data for the government and others, to index all of the names found on official U.S. government federal and state maps, and to ensure uniform geographic names for the federal government.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19831-5)

## Phase 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Phase 1")]

Phase 1 lasted from 1978 to 1981, with a precursor pilot project run over the states of Kansas and Colorado in 1976, and produced 5 databases.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19831,_3-6)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200830-1)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19852-7)
It excluded several classes of feature because they were better documented in non-USGS maps, including airports, the broadcasting masts for radio and television stations, civil divisions, regional and historic names, individual buildings, roads, and [triangulation depot](/w/index.php?title=Triangulation_depot&action=edit&redlink=1 "Triangulation depot (page does not exist)") names.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198318-8)

The databases were initially available on paper (2 to 3 [spiral-bound](/wiki/Coil_binding "Coil binding") volumes per state), on [microfiche](/wiki/Microfiche "Microfiche"), and on [magnetic tape](/wiki/Magnetic_tape_data_storage "Magnetic tape data storage") encoded (unless otherwise requested) in [EBCDIC](/wiki/EBCDIC "EBCDIC") with 248-byte fixed-length [records](/wiki/Record_(computer_science) "Record (computer science)") in 4960-byte [blocks](/wiki/Block_(data_storage) "Block (data storage)").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198519–20-9)

The feature classes for association with each name included (for examples) "locale" (a "place at which there is or was human activity" not covered by a more specific feature class), "populated place" (a "place or area with clustered or scattered buildings"), "spring" (a [spring](/wiki/Spring_(hydrology) "Spring (hydrology)")), "lava" (a [lava flow](/wiki/Lava_flow "Lava flow"), [kepula](/w/index.php?title=Kepula&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kepula (page does not exist)"), or other such feature), and "well" (a [well](/wiki/Well "Well")).[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198320–22-10)
[Mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") features would fall into "ridge", "range", or "summit" classes.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200832-11)

A feature class "tank" was sometimes used for lakes, which was problematic in several ways.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard198625-12)
This feature class was undocumented, and it was (in the words of a 1986 report from the Engineer Topographic Laboratories of the [United States Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Engineers")) "an unreasonable determination", with the likes of [Cayuga Lake](/wiki/Cayuga_Lake "Cayuga Lake") (in upstate New York) being labelled a "tank".[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard198625-12)
The USACE report assumed that "tank" meant "reservoir", and observed that often the coordinates of "tanks" were outside of their boundaries and were "possibly at the point where a dam is thought to be".[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard198625-12)

### National Geographic Names database

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: National Geographic Names database")]

The *National Geographic Names database* (NGNDB[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200830-1) hereafter) was originally 57 computer files, one for each state and territory of the United States (except Alaska which got two) plus one for the District of Columbia.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19833-13)
The second Alaska file was an earlier database, the Dictionary of Alaska Place Names that had been compiled by the USGS in 1967.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19833-13)
A further two files were later added, covering the entire United States and that were abridged versions of the data in the other 57: one for the 50,000 most well known populated places and features, and one for most of the populated places.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19854-14)
The files were compiled from all of the names to be found on USGS topographic maps, plus data from various state map sources.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19833-13)

In phase 1, elevations were recorded in [feet](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)") only, with no conversion to metric, and only if there was an actual elevation recorded for the map feature.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19834-15)
They were of either the lowest or highest point of the feature, as appropriate.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19834-15)
Interpolated elevations, calculated by [interpolation](/wiki/Interpolation "Interpolation") between [contour lines](/wiki/Contour_line "Contour line"), were added in phase 2.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19834-15)

Names were the official name, except where the name contained [diacritic](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") characters that the computer file encodings of the time could not handle (which were in phase 1 marked with an asterisk for update in a later phase).[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19836-16)
Generic designations were given after specific names, so (for examples) [Mount Saint Helens](/wiki/Mount_Saint_Helens "Mount Saint Helens") was recorded as "Saint Helens, Mount", although cities named [Mount Olive](/wiki/Mount_Olive_(disambiguation) "Mount Olive (disambiguation)"), not actually being mountains, would not take "Mount" to be a generic part and would retain their order "Mount Olive".[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19836-16)

The primary geographic coordinates of features which occupy an area, rather than being a single point feature, were the location of the feature's mouth, or of the approximate center of the area of the feature.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19835-17)
Such approximate centers were "eye-balled" estimates by the people performing the digitization, subject to the constraint that centers of areal features were not placed within other features that are inside them.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard19864–5-18)
[alluvial fans](/wiki/Alluvial_fan "Alluvial fan") and [river deltas](/wiki/River_delta "River delta") counted as mouths for this purpose.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19835-17) For cities and other large populated places, the coordinates were taken to be those of a primary civic feature such as the [city hall](/wiki/City_hall "City hall") or [town hall](/wiki/Town_hall "Town hall"), main [public library](/wiki/Public_library "Public library"), main highway intersection, main post office, or [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") regardless of changes over time;[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19835-17)[[a]](#cite_note-19) these coordinates are called the "primary point".[[b]](#cite_note-23)

Secondary coordinates were only an aid to locating which topographic map(s) the feature extended across, and were "simply anywhere on the feature and on the topographic map with which it is associated".[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19835-17)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19857-24)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard19864-25)
River sources were determined by the shortest drain, subject to the proximities of other features that were clearly related to the river by their names.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard19864-25)

### USGS Topographic Map Names database

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: USGS Topographic Map Names database")]

The *USGS Topographic Map Names database* (TMNDB[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19981-26) hereafter) was also 57 computer files containing the names of maps: 56 for 1:24000 scale USGS maps as with the NGNDB, the 57th being (rather than a second Alaska file) data from the 1:100000 and 1:250000 scale USGS maps.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19838-27)
Map names were recorded exactly as on the maps themselves, with the exceptions for diacritics as with the NGNDB.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19839-28)

Unlike the NGNDB, locations were the geographic coordinates of the south-east corner of the given map, except for [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa") and [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam") maps where they were of the north-east corner.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne19838-27)

The TMNDB was later renamed the *Geographic Cell Names database* (GCNDB[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19981-26) hereafter) in the 1990s.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19981-26)

### Generic database

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Generic database")]

The *Generic database* was in essence a machine-readable glossary of terms and abbreviations taken from the map sources, with their definitions, grouped into collections of related terms.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198311-29)

### National Atlas database

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: National Atlas database")]

The *National Atlas database* was an abridged version of the NGNDB that contained only those entries that were in the index to the USGS [National Atlas of the United States](/wiki/National_Atlas_of_the_United_States "National Atlas of the United States"), with the coordinates published in the latter substituted for the coordinates from the former.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198311-29)

### Board on Geographic Names database

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Board on Geographic Names database")]

The *Board on Geographic Names database* was a record of investigative work of the USGS [Board on Geographic Names](/wiki/Board_on_Geographic_Names "Board on Geographic Names")' Domestic Names Committee, and decisions that it had made from 1890 onwards, as well as names that were enshrined by [Acts of Congress](/wiki/Act_of_Congress "Act of Congress").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198313-30)
Elevation and location data followed the same rules as for the NGNDB.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198314-31)
So too did names with diacritic characters.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne198314-31)

## Phase 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Phase 2")]

Phase 2 was broader in scope than phase 1, extending the scope to a much larger set of data sources.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200830-1)
It ran from the end of phase 1 and had managed to completely process data from 42 states by 2003, with 4 still underway and the remaining 4 (Alaska, Kentucky, Michigan, and New York) awaiting the initial systematic compilation of the sources to use.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200830-1)

Many more feature classes were included, including abandoned [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") settlements, [ghost towns](/wiki/Ghost_town "Ghost town"), railway stations on railway lines that no longer existed, [housing developments](/wiki/Housing_development "Housing development"), [shopping centers](/wiki/Shopping_center "Shopping center"), and highway [rest areas](/wiki/Rest_area "Rest area").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200831-2)

The actual compilation was outsourced by the U.S. government, state by state, to private entities such as university researchers.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200830-1)

### Antarctica Geographic Names database

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Antarctica Geographic Names database")]

The *Antarctica Geographic Names database* (AGNDB[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19981-26) hereafter) was added in the 1990s and comprised records for BGN-approved names in Antarctica and various off-lying islands such as the [South Orkney Islands](/wiki/South_Orkney_Islands "South Orkney Islands"), the [South Shetland Islands](/wiki/South_Shetland_Islands "South Shetland Islands"), the [Balleny Islands](/wiki/Balleny_Islands "Balleny Islands"), [Heard Island](/wiki/Heard_Island "Heard Island"), [South Georgia](/wiki/South_Georgia "South Georgia"), and the [South Sandwich Islands](/wiki/South_Sandwich_Islands "South Sandwich Islands").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19981-26)
It only contained records for natural features, not for scientific outposts.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19981-26)

### Additional media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Additional media")]

The media on which one could obtain the databases were extended in the 1990s (still including tape and paper) to [floppy disc](/wiki/Floppy_disc "Floppy disc"), over [FTP](/wiki/File_Transfer_Protocol "File Transfer Protocol"), and on [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM").[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19982-32)
The CD-ROM edition only included the NGNDB, the AGNDB, the GCNDB, and a bibliographic reference database (RDB); but came with database search software that ran on [PC DOS](/wiki/PC_DOS "PC DOS") (or compatible) version 3.0 or later.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19982-32)
The FTP site included extra topical databases: a subset of the NGNDB that only included the records with feature classes for populated places, a "Concise" subset of the NGNDB that listed "major features", and a "Historical" subset that included the features that no longer exist.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSGS19982-32)

## Populated places

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Populated places")]

There is no differentiation amongst different types of populated places.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard198612-33)
In the words of the aforementioned 1986 USACE report, "[a] [subdivision](/wiki/Subdivision_(land) "Subdivision (land)") having one inhabitant is as significant as a major metropolitan center such as [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")".[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeard198612-33)

In comparing GNIS populated place records with data from the [Thematic Mapper](/wiki/Thematic_Mapper "Thematic Mapper") of the [Landsat program](/wiki/Landsat_program "Landsat program"), researchers from the University of Connecticut in 2001 discovered that "a significant number" of populated places in Connecticut had no identifiable human settlement in the land use data and were at road intersections.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcEathronMcGlameryShinSmith20015-34)
They found that such populated places with no actual settlement often had "Corner" in their names, and hypothesized that either these were historical records or were "cartographic locators".[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcEathronMcGlameryShinSmith20015-34)
In [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") in the United States, a "Corner" is a corner of the surveyed [polygon](/wiki/Polygon "Polygon") enclosing an area of land, whose location is, or was (since corners can become "lost"[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBLM198031Lost_corner-35) or "obliterated"[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBLM198037Obliterated_corner-36)), marked in various ways including with trees known as "bearing trees"[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBLM19807Bearing_tree-37) ("witness trees" in older terminology[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBLM198062–63Witness_tree-38)) or "corner monuments".[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBLM198013Corner-39)

From analysing Native American names in the database in order to compile a dictionary, professor William Bright of [UCLA](/wiki/University_of_California_at_Los_Angeles "University of California at Los Angeles") observed in 2004 that some GNIS entries are "erroneous; or refer to long-vanished railroad sidings where no one ever lived".[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBright20043-40) Such false classifications have propagated to other geographical information sources, such as incorrectly classified train stations appearing as towns or neighborhoods on Google Maps.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

## Name changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Name changes")]

The GNIS accepts proposals for new or changed names for U.S. geographical features through [The National Map Corps](/wiki/The_National_Map_Corps "The National Map Corps"). The general public can make proposals at the GNIS web site and can review the justifications and supporters of the proposals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The usual sources of name change requests are an individual state's board on geographic names, or a county board of governors.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownReznikGilat2015-42)
This does not always succeed, the State Library of Montana having submitted three large sets of name changes that have not been incorporated into the GNIS database.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMSL-43)

Name changes may also be made by the President of the United States via an executive order, as was the case with [Executive Order 14172](/wiki/Executive_Order_14172 "Executive Order 14172") 'Gulf of Mexico' being renamed to 'Gulf of America' in 2025.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

Conversely, a group of middle school students in Alaska succeeded, with the help of their teachers, a professor of linguistics, and a man who had been conducting a years-long project to collect Native American placenames in the area, in changing the names of several places that they had spotted in class one day and challenged for being racist, including renaming "Negrohead Creek" to an [Athabascan](/wiki/Athabascan_language "Athabascan language") name Lochenyatth Creek and "Negrohead Mountain" to Tl'oo Khanishyah Mountain, both of which translate to "grassy tussocks" in [Lower Tanana](/wiki/Lower_Tanana_language "Lower Tanana language") and [Gwichʼin](/wiki/Gwich%CA%BCin_language "Gwichʼin language") respectively.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmetzer2012-45)
Likewise, in researching a 2008 book on ethnic slurs in U.S. placenames Mark Monmonier of [Syracuse University](/wiki/Syracuse_University "Syracuse University") discovered "Niger Hill" in [Potter County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Potter_County,_Pennsylvania "Potter County, Pennsylvania"), an erroneous transcription of "Nigger Hill" from a 1938 map, and persuaded the USBGN to change it to "Negro Hill".[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonmonier200833–34-46)

### Removal of racial and ethnic slurs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Removal of racial and ethnic slurs")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | The **[factual accuracy](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute")** of parts of this article (those related to sectiion) **may be compromised due to out-of-date information**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(January 2026)* |

In November 2021, the [United States Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") issued an order instructing that "Squaw" be removed from usage by the U.S. federal government.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaaland2021-47)
Prior efforts had included a 1962 replacement of the "nigger" racial pejorative for [African Americans](/wiki/African_American "African American") with "Negro" and a 1974 replacement of the "Jap" racial pejorative for [Japanese Americans](/wiki/Japanese_American "Japanese American") with "Japanese".[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaaland2021-47)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownReznikGilat2015-42)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENuessel2019188-48)

In 2015, a cross-reference of the GNIS database against the Racial Slur Database had found 1441 racial slur placenames, every state of the United States having them, with California having 159 and the state with the most such names being Arizona.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownReznikGilat2015-42)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENuessel2019188-48)
One of the two standard reference works for placenames in Arizona is Byrd Howell Granger's 1983 book *Arizona's Names: X Marks the Place*, which contains many additional names with racial slurs not in the GNIS database.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownReznikGilat2015-42)[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESTARL2017-49)
Despite "nigger" having been removed from federal government use by [Stewart Udall](/wiki/Stewart_Udall "Stewart Udall"), its replacement "Negro" remained in GNIS names in 2015, as did "[Pickaninny](/wiki/Pickaninny "Pickaninny")", "[Uncle Tom](/wiki/Uncle_Tom "Uncle Tom")", and "[Jim Crow](/wiki/Jim_Crow "Jim Crow")" and 33 places named "niggerhead".[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownReznikGilat2015-42)
There were 828 names containing "squaw", including 11 variations on "Squaw tit" and "Squaw teat", contrasting with the use of "nipple" in names with non-[Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") allusions such as "Susies Nipple".[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrownReznikGilat2015-42)

## Other authorities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Geographic_Names_Information_System&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Other authorities")]

* The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") (USCB) defines *Census Designated Places* as a subset of locations in the National Geographic Names Database.
* [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") (USPS) Publication 28 gives standards for addressing mail. In this publication, the postal service defines two-letter state abbreviations, street identifiers such as boulevard (BLVD) and street (ST), and secondary identifiers such as suite (STE).

The location is determined by the authoritative source and is approved or recognized as official by the U.S. Board on Geographic Names by decision or by policy delegation to the authoritative source. The placing of the location point is governed by policies of the Board as defined in the Names Data Users Guide and GNIS metadata, generally at or near the geographic center, but there are exceptions for certain classes of features. For example, the official feature location of flowing bodies of water (streams, rivers, creeks, etc.) or trending linear features (valleys, gulchs, gullys, hollows, etc.) is at the mouth. A geographic feature may have only one official location regardless of size, extent, composition, structure, or boundaries. The location point is coincident with, but in addition to and independent of, any other geospatial representation or boundary definition that may be attached to the feature in other datasets. Linear and aerial features may have secondary points as defined in the Names Data Users Guide and GNIS metadata. Locations are stored in the Geographic Names Information System as latitude and longitude in decimal degrees to seven places."[[21]](#cite_note-22)
